3: Owing to an increasing demand for poultry meat and egg prod- ucts, poultry production is growing worldwide. In the UK alone, over 950 million chickens and 17 million turkeys are reared each year (DEFRA, 2017). Poultry production generates large quantities of litter, which consists of a mixture of manure, waste bedding and food, as well as feathers. The amount of litter depends on the frequency of its removal. In the EU, the litter is removed with every new flock and substituted with fresh bedding material (Palma and Martin, 2013), while in some countries the same bed- ding is often used for more than one production cycle (Dalólio et al., 2017). The reported amount of poultry litter generated per bird over the production cycle ranges from 1.5 to 5.7/kg of litter/ bird (Dalólio et al., 2017).

Since poultry litter is very rich in plant nutrients nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P) and potassium (K), it has been traditionally utilised as a fertiliser and soil conditioner. However, the effectiveness of

poultry manure as a fertiliser has been questioned due to low C/ P and N/P ratios and the high potential for the loss of N and P through leaching (Vandecasteele et al., 2014). This can lead to an increased concentration of water nutrients, resulting in eutrophi- cation of water bodies and contamination of groundwater (Kelleher et al., 2002). Additionally, spreading fresh manure on land can result in emissions of nitrous oxide, a potent greenhouse gas (GHG), and can affect human and animal health through patho- gens contamination (Billen et al., 2015; Lynch et al., 2013). Alterna- tive strategies to manage poultry litter include composting (Vandecasteele et al., 2014), anaerobic digestion (Rao et al., 2013) and energy recovery through thermal treatment, such as combus- tion (Lynch et al., 2013), pyrolysis (Kim et al., 2009) and gasifica- tion (Palma and Martin, 2013). All these methods have been shown to be viable but each has limitations. For example, compost- ing results in the loss of nitrogen which contributes to emissions of ammonia to the atmosphere or leaching of nitrates to water bodies (Steiner et al., 2010). Similarly, high levels of ammonia in the man- ure can inhibit methane production in anaerobic digestion (Kelleher et al., 2002).

Poultry litter is recognised as a biomass fuel and has a physical appearance and consistency similar to a mixture of woodchips and sawdust. The moisture content of fresh litter can vary between 20% and 50% and its lower heating value from 9 to 13.5 MJ/kg (Dagnall et al., 2000). According to Billen et al. (2015), combustion of poul- try litter has a lower environmental impact compared to land spreading. This has also been observed by Williams et al. (2016) for turkey litter. Combustion also concentrates the phosphorous and potassium in the litter in an inorganic and sterile ash, which can be used as a fertiliser (Billen et al., 2015). Poultry litter is used to produce electricity via direct combustion at several large com- mercial plants in Europe and North America (Wiedemann, 2015). Although thermal treatment processes can be very effective in gen- erating useful energy, thermochemical treatment via gasification provides some advantages over direct combustion, such as better energy recovery (Dalólio et al., 2017). Gasification also offers greater flexibility as it produces syngas, which can either be used in gas engines or boilers for heat and electricity generation or upgraded into methanol and chemical feedstocks via the Fischer- Tropsch process (Sikarwar et al., 2016).

Several biomass gasification facilities are currently in operation in Europe (Sikarwar et al., 2016); however, none of them uses poul- try litter as a feedstock. A couple of studies have assessed the potential of gasification of poultry litter. For example, Priyadarsan et al. (2004) and Taupe et al. (2016) considered updraft gasification, finding that a high alkali content in the feedstock leads to ash agglomeration in the fuel bed. Taupe et al. (2016) reported that updraft gasification has a cold gas efficiency of 26%, which is very low compared to 58.4-79.4% for a fluidised bed (FB) gasifier considered by Palma and Martin (2013). The latter study used chemical equilibrium modelling to design a FB gasifier for on-site (broiler) applications for heat and power generation. According to Dalólio et al. (2017), FB is better than other types of gasifier as it allows the use of a wide range of solid fuels and has a higher pro- duction capacity. FB gasifiers are also flexible with regards to the material in the feed and easy to maintain the temperature below the ash melting point (Pereira et al., 2012).

Although the above and some other studies suggest that poultry litter gasification is a promising technology for energy conversion, its environmental and economic implications are currently unknown. In an attempt to fill this knowledge gap, this paper eval- uates for the first time the environmental impacts and costs of a small-scale poultry litter gasification system coupled with a CHP plant, suitable for applications at broiler farms. The results are compared with other biomass and fossil-fuel alternatives for elec- tricity and heat generation. The methods applied in the study are detailed in the next section, followed by the results and discussion in Section 3 and conclusions in Section 4.

8: The environmental impacts and economic costs have been esti- mated using life cycle assessment (LCA) and life cycle costing (LCC), respectively. The LCA study follows the ISO 14040/44 guide- lines (ISO, 2006a, 2006b), with the system modelling carried out in GaBi V8 (Thinkstep, 2018). The environmental impacts have been estimated using the Recipe 2016 midpoint impact assessment method (Huijbregts et al., 2016). The following 16 impact cate- gories included in this method are estimated: global warming potential (GWP), fossil depletion potential (FDP), freshwater eco- toxicity potential (FETP), freshwater eutrophication potential (FEP), human toxicity potential - cancer & non-cancer (HTPc & HTPnc), ionising radiation potential (IRP), land occupation poten- tial (LOP), marine ecotoxicity potential (METP), marine eutrophica- tion potential (MEP), mineral depletion potential (MDP), ozone

depletion potential (ODP), particulate matter formation potential (PMFP), photochemical oxidants formation potential (POFP), ter- restrial acidification potential (TAP) and terrestrial ecotoxicity potential (TETP). The LCC methodology follows the approach pro- posed by Hunkeler et al. (2008) and Swarr et al. (2011). The goal of the study, data sources and assumptions are detailed in the fol- lowing sections.
9: <header>2.1. Goal and scope of the study </header>

The goal of the study is to quantify the environmental impacts and economic costs of generating heat and electricity from gasifi- cation of poultry litter. The scope is from cradle to grave, including collection and storage of poultry litter and its gasification, syngas cleaning and combustion, production of electricity and heat in a CHP plant and co-production of ash as a fertiliser Construc- tion and decommissioning of the plant are also considered, with the system credited for recycling of metals used for the construc- tion of the plant. The functional unit is defined as 'generation of 1 MWh of energy (0.6 MWh of heat and 0.4 MWh of electricity)'. The study is based on UK conditions.

The litter is fed to a FB gasifier to produce syngas which is then used in a CHP plant to generate heat and electricity. The plant is designed to be suitable for a medium-size broiler farm, assumed to be adjacent to it so that the litter does not leave the site of pro- duction. The litter is collected at the end of each rearing cycle and transferred by a digger to a storage tank. The tank can hold the lit- ter from one rearing cycle which is enough to feed the gasifier for six to seven weeks, whilst the next flock of chicks are growing. Emissions of ammonia and nitrous oxide during the storage have been accounted for following the recommended guidelines (Chadwick et al., 1999; Misselbrook et al., 2016). Assuming an average storage period of 1 month, it is estimated that 290 of ammonia and 80 of nitrous oxide are emitted per tonne of stored litter.

From storage, the litter is fed to the gasifier at a rate of per day. The gasifier contains zeolite and dolomite catalysts, used to reduce tar in the syngas. The gasification process has been simu- lated in ASPEN Plus (2001). For further details on the ASPEN model, see Palma and Martin (2013).

After passing through the gas cleaning system, the syngas is combusted in a kW and kWhth CHP plant to generate electricity and heat. The residual heat from the exhaust gases is recuperated to preheat air for gasification and combustion to increase the plant efficiency. The overall efficiency of the CHP plants is 74%, based on the electrical and thermal efficiencies of 32% and 42%, respectively. Ten percent of the electricity produced is used as a parasitic load to operate pumps, motor and control equipment. The remaining electricity is either used by the farm or exported to the national grid, while the excess heat is used for heating the farm. Assuming 6000 operating hours annually and the plant lifetime of 20 years (IRENA, 2012), the system generates in total 52.6 GWh of energy, of which 21.2 GWh of electricity and 31.4 GWh of heat.

The ash is removed from flue gases by a cyclone and a filter and can be used as a fertiliser in agriculture, replacing industrially- produced potassium and phosphorus fertilisers. The system has been credited for the displacement of these fertilisers using the system expansion approach. The ash composition, including the K2O and P2O5 content, has been obtained from Chastain et al. (2012). Since the potassium and phosphorus in ash available to the plants is typically 50% lower than in synthetic fertilisers due to immobilisation (Pitman, 2006), the credits have been applied accordingly.
Fig. 1. System boundary for poultry litter gasification coupled with combined heat and power (CHP) generation. transport].

13: The composition of the poultry litter can be found in Table S1 in the Supplementary Information (SI) and the inventory data used in LCA and LCC modelling in Table 1. The background LCA data for infrastructure, machinery, transport and other relevant processes have been sourced from Ecoinvent v3.3 (2016).

Capital costs of the plant have been obtained from IRENA (2012) and converted from US$ to British Pounds (£) at the average 2012 exchange rate of 1US$ = £0.65. As the capacity of the plant in the IRENA report is 600 kWe, the costs have been scaled down to 200 kWe considered here. This has been carried out following the approach for the scaling of process plants (Coulson et al., 1993). As a result, it is estimated that a 200 kWe gasification-CHP plant would cost in the region of £1.5 million. Both fixed and variable operational and maintenance (O&M) costs have also been obtained from IRENA (2012). Fixed O&M costs include labour, scheduled maintenance, routine component/equipment replacement (for boilers, gasifiers, feedstock handling equipment and related) and insurance. For biomass power plants, these costs typically range from 1 to 6% of the initial capital costs per year. In the base case, an average value of 3.5% has been assumed; the effect on the results of other values in the range is explored in a sensitivity analysis.

Variable O&M costs, which include non-biomass fuels costs, unplanned maintenance and incremental servicing costs, are assumed at £2.4/MWhe (IRENA, 2012). Considering that poultry lit- ter is a waste, it is assumed that it is available free of charge. The plant would qualify for heat subsidies under the Renewable Heat Incentive (RHI). CHP installations active before 2018 were also eli- gible to receive payments for generating electricity under the Renewables Obligation. However, this scheme is closed for new installations.

The life cycle costs are estimated at the net present value over the lifetime of the system, assuming a discounting rate of 3.5% (HM Treasury, 2013). Electricity and fuel costs are assumed to increase at a rate of 5% per annum (Carbon Trust, 2014), while other operational costs increase with the retail price index (RPI), which was 3% in 2017 (ONS, 2018).

16: Given that the use of biomass for energy is driven primarily by the need to mitigate climate change, the following sections first discuss the results for GWP and then for the other impacts consid- ered in the study.
17: The GWP of 1 MWh of energy generated by poultry litter gasi- fication is estimated at 22.5 kg CO2 eq., including the credits for the avoided emissions (Fig. 2). The credits are related to the avoided production of P2O5 and K20 fertilisers displaced by the ash and end-of-life recycling of metals from the decommissioned plant, which together reduce the total impact by 9.3 kg CO2 eq./MWh. Thus, without the credits, the total impact would be 31.7 kg CO2 eq./MWh.

The main contributors to the GWP are the nitrous oxide emis- sions from the storage of litter (40%, before the credits) and GHG emissions during the operation of the plant (50%). The latter is related to the life cycles of the zeolite catalyst and grid electricity as well as the direct emissions of methane and nitrous oxide from syngas combustion. The rest of the impact is from the construction of the plant (6%), transport (2%) and waste management (1%).

21: The results for the other environmental categories in Fig. 2 show that the operation of the plant is the main contributor (60- 95% before the credits) to all the impacts, except for terrestrial acidification. This is largely due to air emissions from syngas com- bustion (photochemical oxidants), dolomite (mineral depletion) and zeolite (all other impacts). Discharge of the wastewater also accounts for a third of marine eutrophication while the use of grid electricity for the operation of the plant is significant for the ionis- ing radiation potential (43%).

Terrestrial acidification is mainly due to the litter storage because of the ammonia emissions from storage, which con- tribute 67% to the total impact (before the credits). Finally, plant construction causes 15-25% of freshwater eutrophication, human and eco-toxicities and land occupation. All impacts but human toxicity (cancer) benefit from the credits for end-of-life recycling of metals and using ash as a fertiliser, with the freshwater eutrophication, non-cancer human toxicity and ionising radiation potentials being net-negative as the avoided emissions are higher than those from the whole life cycle. The reason that human toxicity (cancer) is not affected is that the credits for ash are counter-balanced by the impact from recycling of steel.

23: <header>3.1.3. Comparison with energy from alternative sources </header>

The energy from poultry litter gasification is compared to the following alternatives (Table S2 in the SI): gasification of waste woodchips; gasification of Miscanthus; natural gas CHP; and elec- tricity from the grid and heat from a natural gas boiler. The first two options are chosen for consideration because they are the main types of bioenergy feedstock and the last two alternatives are currently the main sources of electricity and heat in the UK. The results are shown in Fig. 3. In comparison to these four options, the poultry litter system has the lowest impacts for all the categories but human toxicity, mineral depletion and terrestrial acidification. Its human toxicity (cancer) is 25% higher than that of natural gas CHP, but 40-70% lower than the impact of the other three options. The main source of this impact from the poultry litter system is chromium emitted to freshwater in the production of zeolite. Furthermore, its mineral depletion is three times and 55% higher compared to gas CHP and grid electricity & gas boiler, respectively. As mentioned previously, this is largely due to the use of the dolomite catalyst in the gasifi- cation process. Finally, the acidification potential is 35% and 98% higher than the respective impact of woodchip and Miscanthus gasification due to the ammonia emissions during the storage of litter.

Therefore, compared to the two alternative biomass gasification systems, the poultry litter system is superior for all impact cate- gories, except for terrestrial acidification. This is because they involve additional activities, such as chipping of woodchips and cultivation of Miscanthus. Furthermore, unlike the poultry litter, both feedstocks require transportation to the site. They also pro- duce less ash with a lower concentration of nutrients (Table S2). (Koukouzas et al., 2007) and hence is less likely to be used as fertiliser.

As can be seen in Fig. 3, the impacts which are key drivers for biomass energy systems, such as GWP and fossil depletion, are reduced by more than 90% in the poultry litter system compared to natural gas CHP and grid electricity & gas boiler. Similar reduc- tions are found for freshwater eutrophication, freshwater and mar- line eco-toxicity, non-cancer human toxicity, ionising radiation, ozone depletion and particulate matter. Furthermore, terrestrial ecotoxicity and marine eutrophication are more than 50% lower.

Therefore, gasification of poultry litter offers significant environ- mental benefits over the current fossil-fuel and the two biomass options considered here for most impact categories.

28: <header>3.1.3. Comparison with energy from alternative sources </header>

The energy from poultry litter gasification is compared to the following alternatives (Table S2 in the SI): gasification of waste woodchips; gasification of Miscanthus; natural gas CHP; and elec- tricity from the grid and heat from a natural gas boiler. The first two options are chosen for consideration because they are the main types of bioenergy feedstock and the last two alternatives are currently the main sources of electricity and heat in the UK. The results are shown in Fig. 3. In comparison to these four options, the poultry litter system has the lowest impacts for all the categories but human toxicity, mineral depletion and terrestrial acidification. Its human toxicity (cancer) is 25% higher than that of natural gas CHP, but 40-70% lower than the impact of the other three options. The main source of this impact from the poultry litter system is chromium emitted to freshwater in the production of zeolite. Furthermore, its mineral depletion is three times and 55% higher compared to gas CHP and grid electricity & gas boiler, respectively. As mentioned previously, this is largely due to the use of the dolomite catalyst in the gasifi- cation process. Finally, the acidification potential is 35% and 98% higher than the respective impact of woodchip and Miscanthus gasification due to the ammonia emissions during the storage of litter.

Therefore, compared to the two alternative biomass gasification systems, the poultry litter system is superior for all impact cate- gories, except for terrestrial acidification. This is because they involve additional activities, such as chipping of woodchips and cultivation of Miscanthus. Furthermore, unlike the poultry litter, both feedstocks require transportation to the site. They also pro- duce less ash with a lower concentration of nutrients (Table S2). Miscanthus ash has some potential to be used as fertiliser, with a K2O content of 22% and P2O5 of 6% (Nguyen and Hermansen, 2015), while woodchip ash has very small amounts of nutrients
(Koukouzas et al., 2007) and hence is less likely to be used as fertiliser.

As can be seen in Fig. 3, the impacts which are key drivers for biomass energy systems, such as GWP and fossil depletion, are reduced by more than 90% in the poultry litter system compared to natural gas CHP and grid electricity & gas boiler. Similar reduc- tions are found for freshwater eutrophication, freshwater and mar- line eco-toxicity, non-cancer human toxicity, ionising radiation, ozone depletion and particulate matter. Furthermore, terrestrial ecotoxicity and marine eutrophication are more than 50% lower.

Therefore, gasification of poultry litter offers significant environ- mental benefits over the current fossil-fuel and the two biomass options considered here for most impact categories.
33: The sensitivity analysis explores the influence of the following parameters on the environmental impacts of poultry litter gasification:

<list>the efficiency of the system; 
heat utilisation; 
litter storage time; 
disposal of ash; and 
allocation method for ash used as fertiliser.</list>
The findings are summarised in Fig. 4 and discussed below for each parameter in turn.

3.1.4.1. Efficiency of the gasification system and heat utilisation. It is assumed in the base case that the gasification system is equipped with an energy recovery system, achieving the overall efficiency of 74% (see Section 2.1). However, in the absence of energy recovery, the efficiency of the system would reduce to 60% (Palma and Martin, 2013). In that case, all impacts would increase by 25% com- pared to the base case (Fig. 4). However, poultry litter gasification would still outperform the fossil-fuel alternatives and the other two biomass systems for most of the impacts (see Fig. 3).

It is also assumed in the base case that all available heat in the CHP plant is utilised. However, if there is less demand for heat, then some of it may be wasted. If, for example, only 50% of avail- able heat is used, all impacts would increase by 66% compared to the base case (Fig. 4). Despite that, poultry litter gasification would have lower impacts than the fossil-fuel and biomass alternatives for 12 and 11 categories, respectively.

3.1.4.2. Litter storage time. The effect of changing the duration of lit- ter storage is considered as part of the sensitivity analysis because of the high contribution of ammonia and nitrous oxide emissions to GWP and acidification. For these purposes, the base-case storage

of four weeks is varied between two and six weeks. The results in Fig. 4 show that the GWP and acidification would decrease by 30% and 40%, respectively, if the storage time was reduced to two weeks. Similarly, increasing the storage to six weeks would increase these two impacts by 30% and 40%. Therefore, reducing the storage time would reduce significantly these two impacts. Further reductions can be achieved by drawing the air for gasifica- tion and combustion from the storage ventilation system. This would convert nitrous oxide and ammonia into nitrogen and nitro- gen oxides. However, the latter would increase acidification further as well as eutrophication and photochemical oxidants; further con- sideration of these potential effects is not possible due to a lack of data.

3.1.4.3. Landfilling of ash. In the base case, ash is assumed to be used as a fertiliser. An alternative would be to landfill it. In that case, all impacts would increase significantly (Fig. 4). In particular, freshwater and marine ecotoxicity, as well as freshwater eutroph- ication, would be several orders of magnitude higher. This is mainly due to the additional impacts related to the leachate from the landfilled ash. Moreover, the system no longer benefits from the credits for the displacement of artificial fertilisers as in the base case. As a result, the poultry litter gasification system would have higher impacts compared to natural gas CHP for ten out of 16 impacts (see Fig. 3). However, compared to the other alternatives, poultry litter would have lower impacts for 11 categories.

3.1.4.4. Economic allocation. To gauge the effect of the allocation method on the results, the impacts between electricity, heat and ash (fertiliser) are allocated using economic allocation, instead of system expansion used in the base case. The allocation factors are based on their respective wholesale prices of £44/MWhe (OFGEM, 2017), £32.6/MWhh (BEIS, 2017a) and £50/tash (Table 1). As shown in Fig. 4, changing the allocation method has a significant effect on several impact categories. This includes fossil depletion, ecotoxicities, human toxicity (non-cancer), ionising radiation and land occupation, which are now 2-30 times higher, and GWP, mar- line eutrophication and metal and ozone depletion, which increase by 15-40%. However, for most of the impacts, the poultry litter
gasification would still outperform the biomass and fossil-fuel options.

38: There are no previous LCA studies of poultry litter gasification. However, several studies have assessed biomass gasification of waste wood and Miscanthus which are compared with the equiv- alent systems considered in this work. However, these studies reported only the impacts for electricity generation and used dif- ferent allocation methods for heat and electricity. Therefore, to enable comparison, the results in all the studies, including this one, have been converted to the functional unit of 1 MWh of elec- tricity without allocating any impacts to the heat.

The study by Adams and McManus (2014) considered a waste wood gasification installation in the UK with a capacity of 230 kW. The impacts between electricity and heat were allocated using energy, exergy and economic allocation. The authors found that the GWP ranged from 7 to 15 kg CO2 eq./MWhe with or 21.6 kg CO2 eq./MWhe without allocation. Oreggioni et al. (2017) and Guest et al. (2011) evaluated woodchips and forest residues as gasification feedstocks in Norway and estimated the GWP of 44.3 and 39.6 CO2 eq./MWh. after applying exergy allocation for heat. This translates to 70.3 and 57.4 kg CO2 eq./MWh® without the allocation, respectively. Nguyen and Hermansen (2015) studied Miscanthus gasification in Denmark and used system expansion to credit the system for the heat. They reported the GWP of 26 kg CO2 eq./MWh. with or 40 kg CO2 eq./MWh. without the credits.

The results for waste wood and miscanthus gasification are compared for GWP in Fig. S1 in the SI; poultry gasification is also shown for reference. Comparison of the other impacts is not possi- ble due to different impact assessment methods and/or a lack of other impacts in the other studies. As can be seen in the figure, the GWP estimated here is around three times higher than that in Adams and McManus (2014). However, the results compare well with the findings of Oreggioni et al. (2017) and Guest et al. (2011). A possible reason for the GWP reported by Adams and McManus (2014) being so much lower than in the current and the other two studies is that the authors did not consider the use of zeolite and dolomite catalysts in the gasifier and transport of feedstock.

As can also be seen in Fig. S1, the GWP of Miscanthus gasifica- tion in Nguyen and Hermansen (2015) is significantly lower than in the current study. This is because the authors accounted for CO2 sequestration in soil which has not been considered here.

41: The estimated LCC of the system over its lifetime of 20 years are detailed in Table 2. As can be seen, the capital costs are around £1.5 M and operation and maintenance £1.3 M, with the total sys- tem costs of just under £2.8 M. The cost per MWh of heat and elec- tricity generated is £53.1/MWh without and £27.4/MWh with the revenue. The latter amounts to £1.35 M over the lifetime, compris- ing the income from the sale of ash of £258,000 and the RHI of £1.09 M. Assuming the current wholesale price of electricity from the UK grid of £44/MWh (OFGEM, 2017) and the cost of heat from

a natural gas boiler of £32.6/MWh (BEIS, 2017a), the average annual income/savings from 2630 MWh/yr of heat and electricity generated by gasification of poultry litter would be £111,070/yr. Based on that, the payback period for the system is estimated at 13.5 years. For these estimates, see Table S3 in the SI.
42: <header>3.2.1. Comparison with energy from alternative sources </header>

In this section, the costs of poultry litter gasification are com- pared with a range of alternative sources of energy, with and with- out the income from the RHI, as relevant. In addition to the options considered for the comparison of environmental impacts (Sec- tion 3.1.3), further alternatives considered here are grid electricity and heat from oil and coal as well as heat from grid electricity. Their respective costs are estimated based on the following energy costs for industry (BEIS, 2017): £21.7/MWh for natural gas, £20/ MWh for coal, £44.1/MWh for oil and £142.6 £/MWh for grid elec- tricity. The cost of Miscanthus is assumed at £60/t and woodchips at £120/t (Forest Fuels, 2016; Nix, 2016). The cost of transporting these two feedstocks over 0km is estimated at £7/t (Wang et al., 2012).

The results in Fig. 5 indicate that the unit cost of gasification with the RHI (£27.4/MWh) is much lower than that of any other option considered (£48.6-142.6/MWh). Compared to the other two biomass options, energy from poultry litter has 2-3 times lower costs (Fig. 5). Estimates obtained in this work suggest that neither of these two systems is likely to be paid back within their lifetime (see Tables S4 and S5 in the SI), unless the cost of their feedstocks falls below £20/t.

Even without the RHI, poultry litter gasification is comparable to natural gas CHP and 37-66% cheaper than the other fossil-fuel alternatives. However, without these subsidies, the system would not payback within its lifetime of 20 years. The worst option is grid electricity with electrified heat, with five times higher unit cost (£142.6/MWh) than the equivalent energy form poultry litter gasification.

costs per year in the base case to a typical lowest value of 1% would reduce the payback period to ten years. By contrast, increasing them to the high-end of the scale of 6% would mean that the sys- tem would not be paid back within 20 years.

3.2.2.2. Capacity, capacity factor and heat utilisation. Smaller gasifi- cation plants have higher capital costs per kW installed capacity compared to larger plants as they do not benefit from the econo- mies of scale. This can be seen in Table S6 which shows that a 100 kW plant has a payback time 2.3 times that of a ten-times lar- ger installation (18.2 and 7.9 years, respectively). If the capacity is below 85 kWe, the plant cannot be paid off during its lifetime. This suggests that smaller systems are not economically viable without extra subsidies or a reduction in capital costs.

The capacity factor also influences the costs and the payback period significantly. If it falls from 67% in the base case, corre- sponding to the feed rate of 4 t litter/day, to below 50% (3 t/day), the payback time increases to over 20 years (Table S6). This means that a secure and plentiful feedstock supply is a key requirement for the economic viability of the system.
The costs are similarly sensitive to the amount of heat utilised, with the payback time doubling if only 50% of the available heat is used (Table S6).

3.2.2.3. Feedstock price and renewable heat incentive. Poultry litter is currently available free of charge. If that changes in the future and it becomes chargeable, the cost of its gasification and the payback period would increase proportionally, as shown in Table S6 in SI. For the payback to remain within the 20 year period, the cost of the feedstock must be below £25/t.

As can be seen in Table S6, the RHI also affects the costs and the payback time. If, for example, the RHI is reduced from the current rate of £36.4/MWh to £20/MWh, it would take 17 years to pay off the installation. At £60/MWh, the payback time would reduce to ten years. Withdrawing the subsidy altogether would make gasifi- cation economically unviable, with the payback period longer than the plant's lifetime. This analysis also suggests that the current RHI rate of £36.4/MWh is appropriate for the uptake of the poultry lit- ter gasification technologies.

49: <header>3.3. Implications of poultry litter gasification at the UK level </header>

This section considers the environmental and economic impli- cations of utilising all poultry litter for gasification at the UK level. The amount of the litter produced has been estimated based on the number of birds reared (AHDB, 2017), their litter production rates (Nicholson et al., 2016) and occupancy rates (DEFRA, 2010). The
(M Bq C- (M Annual (kt Cu eq.) (kt N eq.)

latter represents the percentage of the time birds are expected to be inside where litter can be collected. Manure excreted outside is excluded from the calculations as its collection is not feasible.

As indicated in Table S7 in the SI, the total amount of poultry lit- ter produced in the UK is estimated at 4.55 Mt/yr. Of this, only 2.73 Mt produced by broiler chickens, breeding flocks and turkeys is suitable for energy recovery. The litter from laying hens, ducks and other poultry has low calorific value and is not suitable for energy conversion. If all the suitable waste (2.73 Mt) is utilised for energy production via gasification, it could generate around 2 TWh of electricity and 3 TWh of heat (based on the amount of litter needed per 1 MWh in Table 1). Considering the current consump- tion of 350 TWh of electricity (DECC, 2017) and 490 TWh of heat (the latter for space heating) in the UK (DECC, 2014), poultry litter could potentially provide 0.6% of the national electricity and heat demand.

Fig. 6 shows the effect on the environmental impacts of displac- ing the UK grid electricity and heat from gas boilers with the equiv- alent energy from gasification of poultry litter. Except for mineral depletion, which would increase by 56%, all impacts would be reduced by 53% (photochemical oxidants) to 111% (freshwater eutrophication). GWP would decrease by 94%, saving 1.7 Mt CO2 eq. annually or 0.37% of UK's GHG emissions in 2017 (BEIS, 2017a). The demand for fossil fuels would be reduced by 0.7 Mt oil eq., equivalent to the annual fuel consumption of 1 million cars. For context, there were 30.9 million passenger cars in the UK in 2017 (DfT, 2018), consuming 22.7 Mt oil eq. (BEIS, 2018b).

However, to realise these savings, 1900 gasification plants (200 kWe and 268 kWth) would be needed to make use of all avail- able poultry litter. This would require a capital investment of £2.9 bn, with £104 M being required for the annual subsidies through the RHI. As a result, the cost of mitigating climate change through this technological option would be equivalent to £34/t CO2 eq. (see Table S8 in the SI). This compares favourably with the estimated carbon abatement cost in the UK for biogas heat of £56 (CCC, 2015) and onshore wind power of £50/t CO2 eq. (CCC, 2013). There- fore, it is recommended to reinstate the RHI scheme for new installations.

The demand for fossil fuels would be reduced by 0.7 Mt oil eq., equivalent to the annual fuel consumption of 1 million cars. For context, there were 30.9 million passenger cars in the UK in 2017 (DfT, 2018), consuming 22.7 Mt oil eq. (BEIS, 2018b).

However, to realise these savings, 1900 gasification plants (200 kWe and 268 kWth) would be needed to make use of all avail- able poultry litter. This would require a capital investment of £2.9 bn, with £104 M being required for the annual subsidies through the RHI. As a result, the cost of mitigating climate change through this technological option would be equivalent to £34/t CO2 eq. (see Table S8 in the SI). This compares favourably with the estimated carbon abatement cost in the UK for biogas heat of £56 (CCC, 2015) and onshore wind power of £50/t CO2 eq. (CCC, 2013). There- fore, it is recommended to reinstate the RHI scheme for new installations.
52: <header>3.3. Implications of poultry litter gasification at the UK level </header>

This section considers the environmental and economic impli- cations of utilising all poultry litter for gasification at the UK level. The amount of the litter produced has been estimated based on the number of birds reared (AHDB, 2017), their litter production rates (Nicholson et al., 2016) and occupancy rates (DEFRA, 2010). The

latter represents the percentage of the time birds are expected to be inside where litter can be collected. Manure excreted outside is excluded from the calculations as its collection is not feasible.

As indicated in Table S7 in the SI, the total amount of poultry lit- ter produced in the UK is estimated at 4.55 Mt/yr. Of this, only 2.73 Mt produced by broiler chickens, breeding flocks and turkeys is suitable for energy recovery. The litter from laying hens, ducks and other poultry has low calorific value and is not suitable for energy conversion. If all the suitable waste (2.73 Mt) is utilised for energy production via gasification, it could generate around 2 TWh of electricity and 3 TWh of heat (based on the amount of litter needed per 1 MWh in Table 1). Considering the current consump- tion of 350 TWh of electricity (DECC, 2017) and 490 TWh of heat (the latter for space heating) in the UK (DECC, 2014), poultry litter could potentially provide 0.6% of the national electricity and heat demand.

Fig. 6 shows the effect on the environmental impacts of displac- ing the UK grid electricity and heat from gas boilers with the equiv- alent energy from gasification of poultry litter. Except for mineral depletion, which would increase by 56%, all impacts would be reduced by 53% (photochemical oxidants) to 111% (freshwater eutrophication). GWP would decrease by 94%, saving 1.7 Mt CO2 eq. annually or 0.37% of UK's GHG emissions in 2017 (BEIS, 2017a). The demand for fossil fuels would be reduced by 0.7 Mt oil eq., equivalent to the annual fuel consumption of 1 million cars. For context, there were 30.9 million passenger cars in the UK in 2017 (DfT, 2018), consuming 22.7 Mt oil eq. (BEIS, 2018b).

However, to realise these savings, 1900 gasification plants (200 kWe and 268 kWth) would be needed to make use of all avail- able poultry litter. This would require a capital investment of £2.9 bn, with £104 M being required for the annual subsidies through the RHI. As a result, the cost of mitigating climate change through this technological option would be equivalent to £34/t CO2 eq. (see Table S8 in the SI). This compares favourably with the estimated carbon abatement cost in the UK for biogas heat of £56 (CCC, 2015) and onshore wind power of £50/t CO2 eq. (CCC, 2013). There- fore, it is recommended to reinstate the RHI scheme for new installations.
53: This study has evaluated the life cycle environmental and eco- nomic sustainability of poultry litter gasification. The results sug- gest that energy from this system has lower impacts than the fossil-fuel alternatives in 14 out of 16 impact categories. In partic- ular, global warming potential, fossil and ozone layer depletion, freshwater eutrophication, ecotoxicity, human toxicity (non- cancer), ionising radiation and particular matter formation are reduced by more than 90%. Marine eutrophication and terrestrial toxicity are also significantly lower (50%). However, mineral deple- tion would increase by up to three times due to the use of catalysts in the gasification process for removing tar from syngas. Poultry litter gasification also has a lower impact than gasification of woodchips and Miscanthus for all impacts except acidification. The latter, which is largely due to ammonia emissions from the storage of litter, can be reduced by reducing the storage time. This would also result in further savings in the global warming poten- tial due to a reduction in nitrous oxide emissions. These emissions can also be minimised if the air for gasification and combustion is drawn from the storage ventilation system.

The sensitivity analysis shows that most impacts are influenced by the efficiency of the system, heat utilisation, ash disposal method and allocation of impacts. For instance, reduction in the efficiency of the system from 76% to 60% would result in a 25% increase in the impacts. Similarly, if the demand for heat is halved,
the impacts would increase by 66%. However, global warming potential and most other impacts are still significantly lower than for the fossil-fuel and biomass options considered here.

The poultry litter gasification system has a payback period of 13.5 years, taking into account the RHI payments, revenue from the heat and electricity produced and the sale of ash as fertiliser. Even without the subsidies, the cost of generating heat and elec- tricity from poultry litter is 37-66% lower than from fossil fuels. However, without the subsidies, the system would not be viable as the payback period would increase to 25 years. Although the sensitivity analysis suggests that the payback is influenced by cap- ital and feedstock costs, RHI rates and the size and efficiency of the system, gasification is still economically viable under a range of conditions considered in the study.

It is estimated that 4.55 Mt of poultry litter is currently pro- duced annually in the UK, of which 2.73 Mt can be used for energy recovery. Gasifying it could supply 0.6% of the national electricity and heat demand and save 1.7 Mt of GHG emissions per year. The latter is equivalent to around 0.4% of UK annual emissions. Most other impacts would also be reduced, including depletion of fossil resources and the ozone layer as well as toxicity. However, this would require 1900 plants suitable for medium-size poultry farms and a capital investment of £2.9 bn, which could be a signif- icant barrier for the uptake of this technology. In order to encour- age the adoption of this technology, the government should reintroduce the RHI subsidies, especially for waste feedstocks.
